gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Directed evolution (DE) optimizes a target property but may degrade unmeasured properties (e.g., solubility, thermostability). ML-assisted DE uses models myopically focused on the target, exacerbating this trade-off. The paper addresses this by formulating DE as regularized Bayesian optimization, where regularization terms based on evolutionary or structure-based constraints bias designs toward native-like properties.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Proposes regularized Bayesian optimization for DE to balance target optimization with unmeasured properties. Structure-based regularization usually improves designs and never hurts; evolutionary-based regularization is least effective. Regularization focuses search, reducing experimental burden.
process_signals: Problem: DE optimization is under-determined; mutations for target property may harm unmeasured properties.
Solution: Regularized Bayesian optimization with evolutionary or structure-based constraints.
Key finding: Structure-based regularization usually helps, evolutionary-based is less effective.
Impact: Reduces experimental burden compared to model-free methods.
failures_or_limits: Evolutionary-based regularization tends to be least effective.

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: ML-assisted DE improves efficiency but models are myopic, ignoring unmeasured traits. The paper introduces regularized Bayesian optimization using evolutionary (generative models) or structure-based (FoldX) terms to bias toward native-like designs. Evaluated on GB1, BRCA1, Spike. Structure-based regularization usually helps; evolutionary results are mixed. Bayesian approach outperforms prior non-Bayesian ML-assisted DE.
process_signals: Gap: ML-assisted DE models are myopic, only consider target property.
Hypothesis: Regularization with evolutionary or structure-based terms yields better overall designs.
Approach: Bayesian optimization with regularized acquisition functions.
Preliminary results: Structure-based regularization usually better, evolutionary mixed; Bayesian approach reduces wet-lab burden by 67% on GB1 vs. prior work.
methods: Regularization via generative models (TPLM, MRF, HMM) or FoldX
Comparison to traditional DE and unregularized BO
failures_or_limits: Evolutionary regularization leads to worse designs for BRCA1.

section_gists.heading: Directed protein evolution
section_gists.gist: Traditional DE involves iterative mutagenesis, screening, and amplification. It explores design space via parallel search but is expensive and time-consuming. Variants include single mutation walk and recombination.
process_signals: Baseline: Traditional DE is model-free, expensive, and may not account for unmeasured properties.
Motivation: Need for more efficient methods that consider broader constraints.

section_gists.heading: Machine learning-assisted directed protein evolution
section_gists.gist: ML-assisted DE uses screening data to train a surrogate model for in silico screening, reducing experimental burden. However, models are myopic (focus on target property and limited residues). The paper aims to combine strengths of ML-assisted DE (adaptivity) and rational design (whole-sequence consideration) via regularization.
process_signals: Prior work: ML-assisted DE reduces rounds but models are myopic.
Gap: Myopic models ignore unmeasured properties; rational design considers whole sequence but is fixed.
Proposed integration: Adaptive fitness model with whole-sequence regularization.
failures_or_limits: Myopic models may improve target at expense of unmeasured traits.

section_gists.heading: Bayesian optimization
section_gists.gist: Bayesian optimization (BO) models unknown objective with a prior (e.g., GP) and uses acquisition functions to balance exploration/exploitation. Proposed approach adds regularization to acquisition functions to favor native-like sequences.
process_signals: Core methodology: BO with GP prior and acquisition functions (EI, PI, UCB).
Innovation: Custom acquisition functions with regularization terms (evolutionary or structure-based).
methods: Regularization via evolutionary or structure-based factors

section_gists.heading: Generative modeling of protein sequences
section_gists.gist: Evolutionary regularization uses generative models to penalize designs deviating from natural sequences. Primary model: ESM-1b transformer protein language model (TPLM). Also evaluated: profile HMMs and MRFs (GREMLIN). High probability/log-odds under model indicates native-likeness.
process_signals: Hypothesis: Natural sequences have optimized properties; penalizing deviations enforces native-like traits.
Model choice: TPLM captures long-range dependencies; HMM/MRF are fold-family specific.

section_gists.heading: Evolutionary-based regularization factors
section_gists.gist: Uses pre-trained ESM-1b TPLM to compute log-odds scores for variants. Model trained on UniParc with masked language modeling. Also used MRF and HMM for fold-family specific regularization (details in Additional file 1).
process_signals: Implementation: TPLM log-odds as regularization factor.
Alternative models: MRF and HMM explored.

section_gists.heading: Structure-based regularization factors
section_gists.gist: Uses FoldX to compute ΔΔG of mutations relative to wild-type. Protocol: repair PDB, run BuildModel three times, average energy. Negative ΔΔG indicates favorable mutation.
process_signals: Implementation: FoldX ΔΔG as regularization factor.
Structures used: GB1 (2GB1), BRCA1 (1JM7), Spike (6M0J).

section_gists.heading: Bayesian optimization for directed protein evolution
section_gists.gist: BO uses GP regression with squared exponential kernel on one-hot encoded sequences. Hyperparameter (length scale) optimized via log marginal likelihood. GP provides posterior mean and variance for acquisition.
process_signals: Design: GP with RBF kernel, one-hot encoding.
Hyperparameter optimization: maximize log marginal likelihood.

section_gists.heading: Regularized acquisition functions
section_gists.gist: Standard acquisition functions (EI, PI, UCB) are multiplied by a design-specific scaling factor F (log-odds or ΔΔG) to form regularized versions. This shifts selection toward native-like designs.
process_signals: Core innovation: Regularized acquisition functions = standard acquisition × regularization factor.
Expected effect: Shift toward native-like designs.

section_gists.heading: Directed evolution with machine learning and in silico traditional approaches
section_gists.gist: Experiments compare standard ML-assisted DE, regularized BO, and simulated traditional DE (single mutation walk, recombination). GB1 data used for traditional DE simulation due to exhaustive variants. Single mutation walk is deterministic; recombination uses top 3 sequences to create recombinant library.
process_signals: Baselines: Traditional DE (single mutation walk, recombination), unregularized BO.
Simulation details: 191 total variants screened, 20 initial random, 9 batches of 19.
Traditional DE simulation: single mutation walk deterministic; recombination uses Cartesian product of top variants.
failures_or_limits: BRCA1 and Spike data only include single-site mutations, limiting traditional DE simulation to GB1.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Reports results of five DE approaches: single mutation walk, recombination, unregularized BO (GP+EI/PI/UCB), evolutionary regularized BO (GP+EI/PI/UCB+TPLM), and structure-based regularized BO (GP+EI/PI/UCB+FoldX). Each method screened 191 variants over 100 trials with different initializations. 20% data held out.
process_signals: Experimental setup: 191 variants, 20 initial, 9 batches of 19, 100 trials.
Methods compared: traditional DE, unregularized BO, regularized BO (evolutionary and structure-based).
Focus on subset of experiments; additional results in Additional file 1.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Regularization with evolutionary or structure-based terms in BO acquisition functions will yield better overall protein designs by maintaining unmeasured native-like properties.
Structure-based regularization (FoldX) is more reliable than evolutionary-based (TPLM) because it directly models thermodynamic stability.
Bayesian optimization with regularization can outperform both traditional DE and unregularized ML-assisted DE in terms of design quality and experimental efficiency.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Why does evolutionary regularization fail on BRCA1 and Spike? Is it due to model limitations or data characteristics?
How does the choice of acquisition function (EI, PI, UCB) interact with regularization type?
Are there cases where structure-based regularization with UCB is not beneficial?
How sensitive are results to the scaling factor β in UCB?
What are the specific performance metrics (e.g., fitness values, statistical significance) for each method?
Does the method generalize to multi-site mutations beyond the GB1 dataset?
What are the limitations of using FoldX (e.g., accuracy, need for structure) and TPLM (e.g., training data bias)?

events.summary: Directed evolution optimization is under-determined; mutations for target property may harm unmeasured properties.
Regularizing Bayesian optimization with evolutionary or structure-based constraints will yield better designs.
Applied approach to three proteins: GB1, BRCA1, SARS-CoV-2 Spike, evaluating both regularization types.
Structure-based regularization usually leads to better designs and never hurts; evolutionary-based is least effective.
Regularization focuses search, making better use of experimental budget.
Bayesian approach reduces wet-lab burden by 67% on GB1 vs. prior non-Bayesian ML-assisted DE.
Evolutionary regularization leads to worse designs for BRCA1.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains abstract and introduction, clearly stating problem, hypothesis, key results, and method overview.

events.summary: Transformer protein language model (TPLM) ESM-1b used for evolutionary regularization, trained on 250M sequences.
Fold-family specific models (profile HMMs and MRF via GREMLIN) also evaluated for regularization.
GB1 dataset: saturation mutagenesis at four sites (V39, D40, G41, V54), fitness measured by mRNA display.
BRCA1 dataset: single-site mutations in RING domain, E3 ubiquitin ligase activity measured by phage display.
Spike dataset: deep mutational scan of receptor binding domain, ACE2 binding affinity measured by yeast display.
TPLM log-odds score computed by summing final layer logits for each variant sequence.
FoldX used to compute ΔΔG for mutations: repair PDB, run BuildModel three times, average energy.
Bayesian optimization uses GP with squared exponential kernel on one-hot encoded sequences.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk provides detailed data descriptions and modeling steps for evolutionary and structure-based regularization, as well as Bayesian optimization setup.

events.summary: ML-assisted DE outperforms traditional DE on GB1 fitness optimization.
Structure-based regularization usually leads to better designs and almost never hurts.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Directed evolution optimizes a target property but may degrade unmeasured properties (e.g., solubility, thermostability). ML-assisted DE models are myopic, exacerbating this trade-off. The paper addresses this by formulating DE as regularized Bayesian optimization, where regularization terms based on evolutionary or structure-based constraints bias designs toward native-like properties.

canonical_events.summary: Directed evolution optimization is under-determined; mutations for target property may harm unmeasured properties.
Regularizing Bayesian optimization with evolutionary or structure-based constraints will yield better designs by maintaining unmeasured native-like properties.
Design regularized acquisition functions by multiplying standard acquisition (EI, PI, UCB) with a regularization factor (evolutionary log-odds or structure-based ΔΔG).
Select three protein datasets for evaluation: GB1 (binding fitness, 4-site saturation), BRCA1 (E3 ligase activity, single-site mutations), Spike (ACE2 binding affinity, single-site mutations).
Implement evolutionary regularization using ESM-1b TPLM log-odds; also evaluate fold-family specific models (HMM, MRF via GREMLIN).
Implement structure-based regularization using FoldX ΔΔG calculations (repair PDB, BuildModel 3x, average energy).
Compare ML-assisted DE (unregularized BO) against simulated traditional DE (single mutation walk, recombination) on GB1. ML-assisted DE achieves 45% higher fitness.
Evaluate regularized BO methods on all three proteins. Structure-based regularization usually improves designs and almost never hurts; evolutionary-based regularization is unreliable.
Evolutionary regularization via TPLM fails on BRCA1 and Spike, performing worse than unregularized methods.
Structure-based regularization with FoldX is less helpful for GB1 because the structure lacks the binding partner; BRCA1 and Spike structures include binding partners, making FoldX more informative.
Regularization biases variant selection toward favorable regularization scores and concentrates exploration at select residues, as shown by higher residue-specific entropy.
Bayesian approach reduces wet-lab burden by 67% on GB1 compared to prior non-Bayesian ML-assisted DE (191 vs 570 acquisitions).
BRCA1 and Spike datasets only include single-site mutations, limiting traditional DE simulation to GB1 and restricting generalization of multi-site mutation results.
FoldX predictions may be less accurate without complete structural context (e.g., missing binding partner for GB1).
